comments.text: Here I provide an attempt assisted by ChatGPT Pro 5.5. In the document, the main claim is: Let $E=\left\{z\in\mathbb{C}: \left|f(z)\right|<1\right\},$ and \(K=\left\{z\in\mathbb{C}: \left|f(z)\right|\le 1\right\}.\) Let \(E_1,\dots,E_m\) be the connected components of \(E\). For each component, set \(K_j=\overline{E_j}.\) We call a partition of \(\left\{E_1,\dots,E_m\right\}\) an admissible partition. If \(S\) is a block of such a partition, define \( K_S:=\bigcup_{E_j\in S} K_j. \) Let \(n_S\) be the number of roots of \(f\) contained in the components belonging to \(S\), counted with multiplicity, and define the weighted root mean \(c_S=\frac{1}{n_S}\sum_{\alpha\in S} m(\alpha)\alpha.\) Finally define \(r(S)=\sup_{z\in K_S}\left|z-c_S\right|.\) Claim: There exists an admissible partition \(p\) such that \(\sum_{S\in p} r(S)\le 2.\) Consequently, the disks \(\left\{\overline{D}(c_S,r(S)):S\in p\right\}\) cover \(K\) and have total radii at most \(2\). ChatGPT Pro 5.5 is not able to find a counterexample in multiple attempts. (Will still need more verification nonetheless) My approach here is to try to incorporate both results from [511] and [1046]. Hope this helps. PDF ChatGPT link

Thanks. It's unclear to me whether this claim is easier than the problem (it could be harder). You may want to focus on trying to determine whether this is a promising route or not. Credit to GPT-5.5 Thinking for discussion.
